_cite.reason: item_type correction: route item to its true surface by generated-brief substance (Technology folded per operator Option B 2026-06-07)

prior.title: IATA Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Framework and Net Zero Roadmap
prior.summary: IATA's SAF program outlines that sustainable aviation fuel could contribute approximately 65% of emissions reductions needed for aviation to reach net zero CO2 by 2050. The framework emphasizes the critical role of harmonized government policies, technology-agnostic incentives, and production scaling. Mandates are recommended only as part of broader SAF production strategies complemented by innovation and cost reduction programs.
prior.full_brief:
# IATA Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Framework and Net Zero Roadmap

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING | COST ALERT

**The core movement:** SAF production doubled in 2025 but growth is now decelerating sharply, while mandatory blend requirements in Europe and the UK are driving cost spikes that airline cargo carriers — and their freight forwarding supply chains — are absorbing today.

**Production status (2025 baseline):** FACT — "In 2025, SAF output is expected to reach 1.9 million tonnes (Mt) (2.4 billion liters), double the 1 Mt produced in 2024. However, in 2026, SAF production growth is projected to slow down and reach 2.4 Mt." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, "SAF Production Growth Rate is Slowing Down," IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Share of total jet fuel:** FACT — "SAF production in 2025 represents only 0.6% of total jet fuel consumption, increasing to 0.8% the following year." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Cost premium at industry level:** FACT — "At current price levels, the SAF premium translates into an additional USD 3.6 billion in fuel costs for the industry in 2025." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**What triggered deceleration:** FACT — "The estimated SAF output for 2025 of 1.9 Mt is a downward revision from IATA's earlier forecasts due to lack of policy support to take full advantage of the installed SAF capacities." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**EU and UK mandate effect (price distortion):** FACT — "In Europe, ReFuelEU Aviation has sharply increased costs amid limited SAF capacity and oligopolistic supply chains. Fuel suppliers have widened their profit margins to such an extent that airlines pay up to five times more than the price of conventional jet fuel and double the market price of SAF." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Airline premium burden:** FACT — "The cumulative impact of poorly designed policy frameworks is that airlines paid a premium of USD 2.9 billion for the limited 1.9 Mt of SAF available in 2025. Of this, USD 1.4 billion reflects the standard SAF price premium over conventional fuel." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Strategic context — what SAF is meant to do:** FACT — "sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) representing the most impactful solution in the coming decades: it could contribute up to 65% of the reduction in emissions needed by aviation to reach net zero CO2 emissions by 2050." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

**Lifecycle emissions reduction claim:** FACT — "SAF can significantly reduce lifecycle carbon emissions, typically by around 80% compared to conventional aviation fuel." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

**Drop-in compatibility:** FACT — "As a drop-in fuel, it can be blended with conventional aviation fuel and readily used without requiring any modifications to aircraft or airport infrastructure." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating air-primary freight lanes — particularly time-sensitive verticals such as live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, and high-value automotive — the SAF cost premium is already embedded in airline fuel economics and is beginning to appear in fuel surcharge structures on European routes. The 2025 figures confirm this is a present-tense cost pressure, not a future risk.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**IATA (International Air Transport Association):** Represents over 370 airlines accounting for approximately 85% of global air traffic. FACT — "IATA (International Air Transport Association) represents over 370 airlines accounting for some 85% of global air traffic." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

IATA's stated interest is scaling SAF through incentive-based policy rather than mandates, seeking harmonisation across jurisdictions to avoid fragmented compliance burdens. FACT — "Harmonization, simplification, and competition-neutrality are all essential for delivering the greatest possible emissions reductions at the lowest possible cost." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

**ICAO (International Civil Aviation Organization):** The intergovernmental body setting global frameworks. The ICAO Global Framework for SAF — agreed at the Third ICAO Conference on Aviation and Alternative Fuels (CAAF/3) — is built across four building blocks: policy and planning; regulatory frameworks; implementation support; and financing. FACT — "The ICAO Global Framework for Sustainable Aviation Fuels (SAF), Lower Carbon Aviation Fuels (LCAF) and other Aviation Cleaner Energies includes a collective global aspirational Vision to reduce CO2 emissions in international aviation by 5 per cent by 2030, compared to zero cleaner energy use." *Source: ICAO SAF Framework Page, ICAO. https://www.icao.int/SAF

FACT — "The 41st ICAO Assembly adopted a long-term global aspirational goal (LTAG) for international aviation of net-zero carbon emissions by 2050 in support of the UNFCCC Paris Agreement's temperature goal." *Source: ICAO SAF Framework Page, ICAO. https://www.icao.int/SAF

**European regulators (ReFuelEU Aviation):** Have adopted a mandate-first approach (in force from January 2025) that IATA characterises as counterproductive. FACT — "The recent entry into force of ReFuelEU for Aviation (RFEUA) in January 2025 is already presenting significant challenges to aircraft operators in Europe." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

**Fuel suppliers (European market):** IATA identifies an oligopolistic supply structure as a price amplifier. Industry interpretation (IATA position, not an independent finding): FACT — "Fuel suppliers have widened their profit margins to such an extent that airlines pay up to five times more than the price of conventional jet fuel." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

*Analytical inference:* IATA's public critique of EU and UK mandate design is a lobbying signal. Workspaces should read IATA's policy positions as advocacy, not as regulatory authority. The underlying production and cost data, however, are sourced from IATA's own estimates and are consistent with the direction of travel visible from ReFuelEU and CORSIA documentation.

**CORSIA framework:** ICAO's Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation embeds SAF as a compliance mechanism through which aircraft operators reduce their offsetting requirements. FACT — "CORSIA allows aircraft operators to reduce its offsetting requirements through the use of CORSIA eligible fuels, which include CORSIA sustainable aviation fuels and CORSIA lower carbon aviation fuels." *Source: ICAO CORSIA Eligible Fuels Page, ICAO. https://www.icao.int/CORSIA/corsia-eligible-fuels

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Production trajectory — near term:** FACT — 2025 output: 1.9 Mt. 2026 projection: 2.4 Mt, representing a significant slowdown in the year-on-year growth rate from the 2024–2025 doubling. *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Production potential — long term:** FACT — "around 400 Mt of SAF is forecast to be possible to produce in 2050." *Source: IATA SAF Programme Page, IATA (citing Global Feedstock Assessment for SAF Production, September 2025). https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/

**e-SAF mandates — the next conversion trigger:** FACT — "With e-SAF mandates approaching in the UK (2028) and EU (2030), it's essential not to repeat the policy missteps seen with SAF. Already, e-SAF faces a much higher cost base, potentially up to 12 times that of conventional jet fuel." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**e-SAF compliance cost exposure:** FACT — "compliance costs could escalate to EUR 29 billion by 2032 if targets aren't met, as seems very likely with the current policy framework." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**Airline target revision signal:** FACT — "many airlines that have committed to use 10% SAF by 2030 will be forced to reevaluate these commitments. SAF is not being produced in sufficient amounts to enable these airlines to achieve their ambition." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

**CORSIA feedstock expansion (live process):** The ICAO Council is actively expanding the list of CORSIA-approved feedstocks. FACT — "19 November 2025 - ICAO Council approves the classification of new feedstocks and default core LCA values for CORSIA eligible fuels." *Source: ICAO CORSIA Eligible Fuels Page, ICAO. https://www.icao.int/CORSIA/corsia-eligible-fuels

FACT — "27 June 2025 - ICAO Council approves CAEP/13 recommendations, including various amendments to ICAO documents related to CORSIA eligible fuels." *Source: ICAO CORSIA Eligible Fuels Page, ICAO. https://www.icao.int/CORSIA/corsia-eligible-fuels

**IATA Net Zero Roadmaps — framework document update:** FACT — "In September 2024, IATA released updated Policy and Finance Net Zero Roadmaps, containing expanded and deepened analyses." *Source: IATA Net Zero Roadmaps Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/flynetzero/roadmaps/

The roadmaps cover five domains: aircraft technology, energy infrastructure, operations, finance, and policy. FACT — "IATA's Net Zero roadmaps provide step-by-step detailing of critical actions for aviation to achieve net zero CO2 by 2050. They address aircraft technology, energy infrastructure, operations, finance, and policy." *Source: IATA Net Zero Roadmaps Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/flynetzero/roadmaps/

**Peer review process:** FACT — "A peer-to-peer review, complemented by a modeling tool provided by the Air Transportation Systems Laboratory at University College London, was conducted to calculate emission reductions for each technology." *Source: IATA Net Zero Roadmaps Page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/flynetzero/roadmaps/

*Analytical inference:* The combination of decelerating SAF production growth and advancing e-SAF mandate dates (UK 2028, EU 2030) creates a structural cost escalation corridor for European-route air freight between now and 2032. The EUR 29 billion compliance cost figure, if borne even partially by air carriers, is large enough to trigger structural fuel surcharge redesign by major carriers on transatlantic and intra-European corridors.

**MONITORING — re-check window:** Review this signal at Q2 2026 for European Commission STIP (Strategic Technologies for Europe Platform) timeline announcement, referenced by IATA as a step forward without a clear timeline. Re-check again at Q4 2027 ahead of the UK e-SAF mandate effective year of 2028.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

COST ALERT

**Air-primary operations — most directly exposed.** For workspaces moving high-value, time-sensitive cargo (live events equipment, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV production materials, high-value automotive components) predominantly by air, SAF cost passthrough is the operative mechanism:

- *Operational implication:* Carriers operating on European routes are already paying the SAF premium embedded in ReFuelEU Aviation from January 2025. That premium — sourced at up to 5× conventional jet fuel on mandated routes — feeds into airline cost bases that surcharge structures reflect. Freight forwarding workspaces quoting on European air routes should model SAF surcharge increases as a recurring line item, not a one-off.

- *Operational implication:* The SAF price multiple of 2× the open-market SAF price (and 5× conventional jet fuel) in mandated European markets is not a temporary spike. It reflects structural supply concentration and will persist until additional production capacity reaches final investment decision and commercial operation — a timeline that, based on the 2026 growth projection, extends well beyond 2026.

**e-SAF cost base — forward-planning horizon:** FACT — e-SAF faces "a much higher cost base, potentially up to 12 times that of conventional jet fuel." *Source: IATA Press Release No. 57, IATA, 9 December 2025. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2025-releases/2025-12-09-04/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces with clients who have committed to scope 3 emission reduction targets linked to air freight will face increasing pressure to produce SAF-attributable emissions data as SAF mandates formalise carrier reporting requirements. The CORSIA monitoring, reporting, and verification (MRV) framework already sets standards for how SAF emissions reductions are accounted at the aircraft operator level — this is the upstream source of the data that freight Scope 3 reporting will eventually need to draw from.

**Non-EU/UK routes — lower immediate cost exposure but not zero:** *Analytical inference:* Routes outside the EU and UK ReFuelEU mandate zone are not currently subject to the same mandatory blending requirements. However, CORSIA Phase 1 (2021–2026 baseline) applies globally to participating international operators, and the ICAO aspirational 5% CO2 reduction by 2030 sets the direction of travel for non-EU jurisdictions as well. Workspaces with significant transpacific or transatlantic non-EU air freight should monitor US SAF policy developments separately, as US production incentive structures differ materially from EU mandate structures.

**Cargo vertical — live events and film/TV:** *Operational implication:* These verticals move equipment on tight charter and ad-hoc air freight schedules that cannot easily absorb last-minute fuel surcharge changes. SAF surcharge transparency (what the carrier is charging, what it reflects, how it is calculated) should be a standard clause in spot-rate negotiations and master rate agreements with air carriers operating EU-origin or EU-destination flights.

**Cargo vertical — fine art and luxury goods:** *Operational implication:* Clients in these verticals increasingly require sustainability credentials as part of carrier selection and shipment documentation. SAF certificate provenance (which sustainability certification scheme, which feedstock category, which lifecycle value under CORSIA) is becoming a legitimate client-facing data point. Workspaces without access to this data from their carrier partners are exposed to being unable to substantiate emissions claims in client sustainability reporting.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

COMPETITIVE EDGE

**No sourced data is available in the provided source blocks on named competitor freight forwarder positions regarding SAF procurement, SAF surcharge strategies, or client-facing SAF certificate programmes.** Specific competitor positioning requires carrier-specific and forwarder-specific data not available in the provided sources.

*Analytical inference:* The competitive dynamic is structural rather than competitor-specific at this stage. Freight forwarders who have established SAF certificate procurement relationships — either through direct airline SAF offtake agreements or through IATA's CADO (Civil Aviation Decarbonization Organization) registry — are positioned to provide clients with verifiable, CORSIA-compliant SAF attribution data. Those without such relationships cannot credibly make SAF-linked emissions claims in client reporting.

*Analytical inference:* The IATA SAF Matchmaker tool (referenced in source content as a CADO-operated platform) is the established mechanism for buyers and sellers to connect in the SAF certificate market. Workspaces not tracking this tool are operating with a gap in their procurement intelligence.

*Analytical inference:* The deceleration in SAF production growth, combined with mandate-driven price spikes, means that early SAF offtake agreement holders are relatively better positioned than spot buyers. The workspaces most exposed are those relying entirely on spot-rate air freight without any long-term carrier relationships that include SAF transparency provisions.

**European route concentration risk:** Workspaces with a high proportion of EU-origin or EU-destination air freight are more directly exposed to the ReFuelEU Aviation cost dynamic than those operating primarily on non-European corridors. This is a differentiator in competitive quoting: a workspace that can explain the SAF surcharge origin and trajectory is more credible than one that cannot.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

1. SAF production reached 1.9 million tonnes in 2025 — double the 2024 level — but remains under 1% of total jet fuel use. This is a verified IATA figure (December 2025).

2. On European routes, SAF mandate costs (ReFuelEU Aviation, in force January 2025) are already flowing through airline cost bases and appearing in surcharge structures. The premium on mandated European routes has reached up to 5× the conventional jet fuel price — this is the source of the fuel surcharge increases your clients are seeing on EU air freight movements.

3. SAF is a drop-in fuel — it does not require changes to aircraft or airport infrastructure. The barrier is cost and production volume, not technical compatibility.

4. SAF offers up to approximately 80% lifecycle carbon reduction versus conventional jet fuel. This is the IATA figure; it requires sustainability certification scheme validation to be attributable to a specific shipment.

5. ICAO's CORSIA framework sets the global standard for SAF sustainability criteria and lifecycle accounting. SAF certificates that are CORSIA-compliant have a defined, audited emissions reduction value.

**Questions to pose to carrier partners:**
- Which sustainability certification scheme certifies your SAF supply?
- What feedstock category and lifecycle emissions value applies to the SAF on this route?
- Is the SAF surcharge a passthrough of your actual SAF purchase cost, or a fixed programme fee?
- Can you provide CORSIA-eligible fuel batch documentation for Scope 3 reporting purposes?

**Claims to avoid:**
- Do not characterise SAF as a near-term solution to aviation's full emissions footprint. At 0.6% of jet fuel use in 2025, it is a trajectory, not a solution.
- Do not represent a specific lifecycle emissions reduction figure for a client's shipments without the underlying certification documentation from the carrier.
- Do not state that SAF "offsets" emissions — the CORSIA framework credits SAF as an emissions reduction, not an offset. The distinction matters for Scope 3 reporting integrity.
- Do not quote the IATA 65% contribution figure as a current fact — it is a modelled 2050 projection, not a present-day operational figure.

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Request SAF transparency data from air carrier partners on European routes.**
European routes are under the ReFuelEU Aviation mandate (in force January 2025). Carriers on these routes are blending SAF and passing costs through. The workspace should, as a standard account management action, request from each carrier: the sustainability certification scheme name, feedstock category, and CORSIA lifecycle emissions value for SAF used on routes where surcharges are applied. This data is needed for any client Scope 3 reporting that references air freight emissions. Timeline: immediate. Effect on verticals: fine art, luxury goods, live events, film and TV — all of which have clients with sustainability disclosure expectations.

**Action 2 — Track the IATA CADO registry and SAF Matchmaker for certificate market access.**
The Civil Aviation Decarbonization Organization (CADO), launched by IATA, operates the SAF certificate registry and Matchmaker tool. Workspaces without a working knowledge of this mechanism cannot engage credibly in SAF certificate procurement discussions with clients or carriers. Assign a sustainability-designated staff member to review CADO registry activity on a quarterly basis.

**Action 3 — Add SAF surcharge transparency language to carrier rate agreements.**
Current rate agreements with air carriers on EU-origin and EU-destination routes should be reviewed for fuel surcharge definitions. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether existing agreement language adequately captures SAF-specific surcharge passthrough, how disputes over SAF surcharge quantum are resolved, and what documentation rights the workspace holds requires counsel review against specific agreement terms.

**Action 4 — Monitor UK e-SAF mandate timeline (2028 effective date).**
The UK e-SAF mandate takes effect in 2028. For workspaces with significant UK-origin or UK-destination air freight, this is a 24-month planning horizon. The cost base for e-SAF is sourced at up to 12× conventional jet fuel — materially higher than current SAF premiums. Begin scenario modelling now for what a UK e-SAF surcharge would do to landed costs on the workspace's highest-volume UK air lanes.

**Action 5 — Do not commit to SAF-linked client sustainability claims without supply chain documentation.**
Multiple airlines have indicated they will need to revise their own 10% SAF by 2030 commitments. Workspaces should not make forward SAF availability commitments to clients that depend on airline SAF commitments that are themselves under review.

---

Its headline figure (1.9 Mt in 2025) is corroborated verbatim by the December 2025 press release. No content from the fact sheet URL has been used as a FACT source.*

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1-7) | Why this source matters |
|-------------|-----|---------------------|------------------------|
| IATA SAF Programme Page (canonical hub) | https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/sustainable-aviation-fuel-saf/ | 4 | Primary IATA hub for SAF production data, feedstock pathways, CADO registry, and SAF Matchmaker tool — essential for ongoing production tracking and certificate market intelligence. |
| IATA Net Zero Roadmaps (five-domain) | https://www.iata.org/en/programs/sustainability/flynetzero/roadmaps/ | 4 | Contains the only available peer-reviewed (UCL-modelled) step-by-step roadmap to 2050 net zero across aircraft technology, energy, operations, finance, and policy — foundational for any forward planning brief on aviation decarbonisation trajectory. |
| ICAO Global Framework for SAF, LCAF and Aviation Cleaner Energies | https://www.icao.int/SAF | 3 | Intergovernmental source for the four-building-block SAF framework agreed at CAAF/3 and the 5% CO2 reduction by 2030 aspirational goal — the highest-authority non-binding global policy reference for SAF. |
| ICAO CORSIA Eligible Fuels Page | https://www.icao.int/CORSIA/corsia-eligible-fuels | 3 | The authoritative ICAO source for CORSIA SAF sustainability criteria, feedstock classifications, and lifecycle emissions values — directly relevant to any Scope 3 SAF attribution claim a workspace or its carrier partners make. |
